result:
# The Weight of Ordinary Days

There's a Tuesday afternoon I keep returning to. Not because anything happened—that's exactly the point. I was sitting in a laundromat watching clothes tumble in the dryer, and for maybe three minutes, I wasn't performing anything for anyone. I wasn't crafting a version of myself. I was just there, existing alongside the hum of machines and the smell of warm cotton.

I think about how rare that is now. Not the solitude—we have plenty of that—but the unselfconsciousness. The not-narrating. Somewhere along the way, we became the authors of our own lives in real time, composing the story as we live it, which means we're never quite living it. We're always half a step behind ourselves, turning experience into content, into anecdote, into something that justifies having happened.

I used to think the examined life was the highest aspiration. Socrates said the unexamined life wasn't worth living, and I took that as gospel for years. But I've started to wonder if there's a point where examination becomes its own pathology. Where you're so busy holding your life up to the light, turning it this way and that, checking for meaning, that the living part quietly slips out the back door.

---

My grandfather never journaled. Never meditated. Never once, as far as I know, talked about his "journey" or described himself as a work in progress. He woke up, made coffee in a percolator that took too long, drove to a job he neither loved nor hated, came home, read the paper, and watched the yard go dark. He did this for forty years.

And yet there was something in him that I find missing in the most optimized, most intentional, most curated lives I see now—including, sometimes, my own. A settledness. Not complacency, not resignation. Something more like gravity. He knew where he was. He wasn't trying to be anywhere else.

I'm not romanticizing that. He had his silences that were probably loneliness. His routines that were probably ruts. He missed things he should have seen because he wasn't trained to look inward the way we are. But I wonder if we've over-corrected. If in gaining fluency in the language of self, we lost the ability to simply sit in a room without asking what the room means.

---

Here's what I've been circling around: I think we're terrified of the ordinary. Not bored by it—terrified of it. Because if a Tuesday afternoon in a laundromat is just a Tuesday afternoon in a laundromat, then maybe a life is just a life. Maybe it doesn't arc. Maybe it doesn't build toward revelation. Maybe the moments we're waiting for—the ones that are supposed to crack everything open and show us what it was all for—maybe they don't come. Or maybe they come and we don't recognize them because they look too much like everything else.

That possibility is unbearable. So we frame and filter and narrate. We turn jogs into training. Meals into experiences. Friendships into networks. Reading into growth. We make every input an investment and every output a return, and the whole machinery of a human life becomes a kind of engine pointed at some future version of ourselves that will finally—finally—be the finished product.

But the finished product doesn't exist. That's the secret everyone knows and no one absorbs. You don't arrive. The person you're becoming is also becoming someone else, endlessly, and the moment you think you've reached the clearing, the trees close in again and you're back in the middle of it, ordinary and bewildered and carrying a bag of groceries up the stairs.

---

I want to make a case for bewilderment. For the grace of not knowing what your life is about while you're in it. For letting a day be shapeless. For the radical act of sitting somewhere—a bench, a laundromat, a porch—and not extracting anything from the experience. Not a lesson. Not a photo. Not even a memory, really. Just the thing itself, unmetabolized.

There's a Mary Oliver line I come back to: "Attention is the beginning of devotion." I used to read that as a call to mindfulness, to the Instagram version of presence—notice the dew on the leaf, be here now. But I think she meant something wilder. Attention that doesn't serve you. Attention that isn't a practice or a discipline. Attention that is, itself, the point. The dew on the leaf doesn't care if you see it. Your seeing it doesn't make you better. But the seeing—the pure, useless act of it—is the closest thing to prayer I understand.

---

My grandfather died on a Wednesday. An ordinary day. The percolator was still on the counter. The paper was on the table, folded to the crossword he'd half finished. Seven across was left blank. I don't know what the clue was.

I think about that blank space sometimes. The unfinished thing. How a life just stops mid-word, mid-gesture, and there's no final chapter that ties it together. There's just the Tuesday, and then the Wednesday, and then the silence where Thursday would have been.

And I think maybe that's not tragedy. Maybe that's the truest thing. That we are, all of us, mid-sentence. That the ordinary days aren't the filler between the meaningful ones. They are the meaningful ones. We just can't see it because we're inside them, and they look like nothing, and they are everything, and they end.
